sections.title: SKILL 11-13

parts.title: SKILL 11: LISTEN FOR EXPRESSIONS OF AGREEMENT
parts.instruction: In this exercise, listen carefully to each short dialogue and question on the recording, and then choose the best answer to the question. You should pay attention to expressions of agreement.

questions.passage:
(man) I'd like to take a trip down the coast this weekend.
(woman) Me, too.
questions.question: 1. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN MEAN?

options.text: The trip would cost too much.
She doesn't think that a trip would be a good idea.
She would like to take two trips rather than one.
She would also like to take a trip.

questions.passage:
(woman) I would like to see Matt elected to the town council next month.
(man) So would I.
questions.question: 2. WHAT DOES THE MAN MEAN?

options.text: He would like to see the elections for town council.
He agrees that Matt should be elected.
He thinks the elections should take place next month.
He disagrees with the woman.

questions.passage:
(man) I'm not sure if I should take begin ning or intermediate French next semester.
(woman) Neither am I.
questions.question: 3. WHAT DOES THE W7OMAN MEAN?

options.text: She is not sure which course she should take.
She's not sure if she should take a trip to France.
She knows that she is not ready for intermediate French.
She wants to take neither beginning nor intermediate French.

questions.passage:
(man) The food in the cafeteria is not exactly the best food I have ever tasted.
(woman) You can say that again!
questions.question: 4. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN MEAN?

options.text: The man should repeat what he said.
The man said something foolish.
She thinks that the food is the best she has ever tasted.
She agrees that the food is pretty bad.

questions.passage:
(woman) This party certainly has been fun!
(man) I'll say!
questions.question: 5. WHAT DOES THE MAN MEAN?

options.text: This party hasn't been any fun at all.
He wonders if the woman enjoyed herself.
He wants to know what she said.
He's enjoyed himself tremendously.

questions.passage:
(man) I'm not about to condone what she did!
(woman) I'm not either.
questions.question: 6. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN MEAN?

options.text: She condones what happened.
She does not like what the man said.
She agrees with the man about what happened.
She says that she did not do it.

questions.passage:
(woman) Those people upstairs always have such loud parties.
(man) Don't they!
questions.question: 7. WHAT DOES THE MAN MEAN?

options.text: He thinks the parties aren't loud.
He says that the neighbors don't have many parties.
He agrees that the upstairs neighbors are noisy.
The loud parties don't bother him.

questions.passage:
(man) This meal is really delicious!
(woman) Isn't it!
questions.question: 8. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN MEAN?

options.text: She doesn't like this meal too much.
This food tastes wonderful to her.
She's not sure if she likes it.
She can't stand this meal.

questions.passage:
(man) I can't imagine what he was thinking about when he bought that car.
(woman) Neither can I!
questions.question: 9. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN MEAN?

options.text: She agrees that getting the car was not a good idea.
She imagines that she would like to have a similar car.
She thinks that the man is mistaken about the car.
She thinks the man has no imagination.

questions.passage:
(woman) This should be a two-semester course.
(man) One semester is just not enough time to learn all the material.
questions.question: 10. You can say that again! WHAT DOES THE MAN MEAN?

options.text: He would like the woman to repeat what she said.
He thinks that one semester is enough time for the course.
He also thinks that the course should be extended.
He would like to take the course two semesters from now.

parts.title: SKILL 12: LISTEN FOR EXPRESSIONS OF UNCERTAINTY AND SUGGESTION
parts.instruction: TOEFL EXERCISE 12: In this exercise, listen carefully to each short dialogue and question on the recording, and then choose the best answer to the question. You should be particularly careful of expressions of uncertainty and suggestion.

questions.passage:
(woman) Are we supposed to read all ten chap ters before the exam?
(man) As far as I can tell, we are.
questions.question: 1. WHAT DOES THE MAN MEAN?

options.text: He's sure about which chapters they are to read.
He thinks he knows what the assignment is.
He has to tell her how far she should go.
The professor told them to read the chapters after the exam.

questions.passage:
(man) You're out of apple pie!
(woman) Sorry. Why not try the cherry pie?
questions.question: 2. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN SUG GEST?

options.text: The man should take the pie out.
The man should try something else.
The man shouldn't try cherry pie.
The man should feel sorry.

questions.passage:
(woman) Do you know when the movie starts?
(man) It starts at 8:00, doesn't it?
questions.question: 3. WHAT DOES THE MAN MEAN?

options.text: He knows the movie starts at 8:00.
He is not quite sure when the movie begins.
He thinks the start of the movie has been changed.
He will start the movie himself at 8:00.

questions.passage:
(woman) We can't leave now. We have to do the dishes.
(man) Let's leave the dishes until later.
questions.question: 4. WHAT DOES THE MAN SUGGEST?

options.text: Not doing the dishes now .
Leaving the house with the dishes .
Leaving later so that they can do the dishes now .
Washing the dishes before they leave .

questions.passage:
(man) Do you think Matt has enough talent for the role?
(woman) As far as I can tell.
questions.question: 5. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN MEAN?

options.text: She's told Matt he'll go far.
Matt has far from enough talent.
She told Matt to roll farther.
She believes Matt has the ability for the part.

questions.passage:
(woman) Did you hear that Mary's in the hospi tal with a broken leg?
(man) Let's go visit her.
questions.question: 6. WHAT DOES THE MAN SUGGEST?

options.text: They should go to the hospital.
Mary should visit the man.
The woman should try not to break her leg.
They should go on a trip with Mary.

questions.passage:
(man) Where are the children?
(woman) They've gone to the park to play ball, haven't they?
questions.question: 7. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN MEAN?

options.text: She knows where the children are.
The children have finished playing ball.
She's going to the park to find the children.
She believes that the children are in the park.

questions.passage:
(man) I need some graph paper for my math assignment.
(woman) I'm all out. Why don't you check with
questions.question: 8. Tom next door? WHAT DOES THE WOMAN SUG GEST?

options.text: The man should try to borrow some from a neighbor.
The man should take a check to Tom.
The man should work on his math assignment with Tom.
The man should check behind the door.

questions.passage:
(woman) Is the utilities bill due on the first or the fifteenth?
(man) On the fifteenth, as far as I know.
questions.question: 9. WHAT DOES THE MAN MEAN?

options.text: He thinks the bill is due in the middle of the month.
The bill is approximately fifteen dollars.
He knows when they should pay the bill.
The bill is going to be fifteen days late.

questions.passage:
(man)  We need to decide whether or not we are going to buy that house.
(woman) It's such a big decision. Let's sleep on it before we decide.
questions.question: 10. WHAT DOES THE WOMAN SUG GEST?

options.text: They should postpone their decision until morning.
They should go to sleep in the new house.
They should not buy such a big house.
They should decide where to go to sleep.

parts.title: SKILL 13: LISTEN FOR EMPHATIC EXPRESSIONS OF SURPRISE
parts.instruction: TOEFL EXERCISE 13: In this exercise, listen carefully to each short dialogue and question on the recording, and then choose the best answer to the question. You should be particularly careful of expressions of emphatic surprise.

questions.passage:
(man)         Greg should be here any moment.
(woman)       Then, he is coming to the party!
questions.question: 1.       WHAT HAD THE WOMAN ASSUMED?

options.text: Greg always comes to parties.
Greg would come to the party later.
Greg was unable to attend the party.
Greg would stay at the party for only a moment.

questions.passage:
(woman)       My motorcycle is over there. That's how I got to school today.
(man)         So you can ride a motorcycle.
questions.question: 2.       WHAT HAD THE MAN ASSUMED?

options.text: The woman always rode her motorcycle to school.
The woman was not coming to school today.
The woman was an expert motorcycle rider.
The woman did not know how to ride a motorcycle.

questions.passage:
(man)         I'm having a few friends over for din ner tonight. Would you like to come?
(woman)       So you do know how to cook!
questions.question: 3.       WHAT HAD THE WOMAN ASSUMED?

options.text: The man was not a very good cook.
The man never invited friends over for dinner.
The man would never invite him over for dinner.
The man was an excellent cook.

questions.passage:
(woman)       I'm so tired from all that exercise.
(man)         Then, you did run three miles this morning.
questions.question: 4.       WHAT HAD THE MAN ASSUMED?

options.text: The woman had run more than three miles.
The woman always got lots of exercise.
The woman ran for three hours in the morning.
The woman had not gotten much exercise.

questions.passage:
(man)         I just spent five hours working on my research project.
(woman)       Bob, you were in the library.
questions.question: 5.       WHAT HADTHE WOMAN ASSUMED ABOUT BOB?

options.text: He had been somewhere else.
He had been in the library.
He had been working on his research project.
He would start working on his project in five hours.

questions.passage:
(man)         I just finished carrying the last piece of furniture.
(woman)       Then, you have moved into a new apartment!
questions.question: 6.       WHAT HAD THE WOMAN ASSUMED ABOUT THE MAN?

options.text: He had changed apartments.
He did not like his new apartment.
He was still in his old apartment.
He had moved from a house to an apartment.

questions.passage:
(woman)       Would you like to share some of this piece of chocolate cake?
(man)         So you do eat sweets.
questions.question: 7.       WHAT HAD THE MAN ASSUMED?

options.text: The woman did not like desserts.
The woman ate sweets regularly.
The woman would not share her chocolate cake.
The woman had eaten his piece of cake.

questions.passage:
(man)         I'm studying now for my driver's license test.
(woman)       Then, you will take the test this after noon.
questions.question: 8.       WHAT HAD THE WOMAN ASSUMED?

options.text: The man was going to study hard.
The man already had a driver's license.
The man would not take the test.
The man had already taken the test.

questions.passage:
(woman)       I'm heading off to physics class now.
(man)         Then you did register for that course!
questions.question: 9.       WHAT HAD THE MAN ASSUMED ABOUT THE WOMAN?

options.text: She had registered in physics.
She would go to physics class later.
She had already taken a physics class.
She had not enrolled in physics.

questions.passage:
(woman)       The plumber just left.
(man)         Then the pipes have been cleared.
questions.question: 10.      WHAT HAD THE MAN ASSUMED?

options.text: The pipes were not clear.
The plumber would be late.
The plumber had already cleared the pipes.
The pipes did not need to be cleared.

sections.instruction: Expressions of agreement are common in the short dialogues, so you should become familiar with them. The following example shows agreement with a positive statement.
